summary:
The Third Dynasty of Ur, known to historians as Ur III, flourished approximately 2112–2004 BC and represents the last great Sumerian renaissance before the Semitic Amorite kingdoms came to dominate Mesopotamia. Founded by Ur-Nammu after the expulsion of the [[event:1730|Gutian invaders]], the dynasty created the most thoroughly bureaucratic state the ancient world had yet seen, governing a territory that encompassed much of modern Iraq and parts of Iran and Syria through a vast network of administrators, scribes, and record-keepers.

Ur-Nammu, the dynasty's founder, is known for promulgating the [[event:1725|Code of Ur-Nammu]], the oldest surviving law code in world history — predating the more famous Code of Hammurabi by three centuries. He also commissioned the construction of the [[event:1726|Great Ziggurat of Ur]], a monumental stepped temple platform that became one of the defining architectural symbols of Mesopotamian civilisation. His son and successor Shulgi completed the ziggurat and dramatically expanded the administrative apparatus of the state, creating a system of standardised weights, measures, calendars, and scribal training that unified economic life across the empire.

Under [[event:3731|Shulgi of Ur]], who reigned for 48 years, the Ur III state reached its administrative peak. Shulgi promoted himself as a divine king, a claim he reinforced through athletic feats, musical accomplishment, and extensive religious patronage. The state he governed was organised through a system of provincial governors (ensis) who delivered standardised tribute payments recorded in clay tablets. Hundreds of thousands of these administrative tablets survive, providing historians with an extraordinarily detailed picture of the economy, labour organisation, and political geography of the ancient Near East.

The Ur III period also saw a flowering of Sumerian literary culture. Hymns, laments, mythological narratives, and wisdom literature were composed and copied in the schools (edubba) attached to temple institutions. The administrative efficiency of the state supported large numbers of scribes, whose training involved copying earlier literary texts and thus preserving and transmitting much of the Sumerian literary tradition that survives today.

The dynasty collapsed around 2004 BC under a combination of pressures: Amorite tribal groups from the northwest had been encroaching on settled Mesopotamian territories for decades, and the Elamites from the east invaded and sacked Ur itself, taking the last Ur III king captive. This catastrophic end was mourned in a famous literary composition known as the [[event:3729|Lament for Ur]], which describes the city's destruction and the abandonment of its patron deity Nanna. Though the Ur III state was gone, its administrative legacy shaped subsequent Mesopotamian civilisations for centuries.
